name: Complement & 'at least one'
description: The 'at least one' trick: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none). Dice and coins, de Méré's paradox, and the expected-count-vs-probability trap.
primer:
## 'At least one' = 1 − 'none'
Directly summing the ways to get **at least one** success double-counts (one trial succeeding doesn't exclude another). The clean route is the **complement**:

$$P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none}).$$

For **independent** trials the 'none' case factorises:

$$P(\text{none}) = \prod_i (1 - p_i).$$

- **One die, n rolls, at least one 6:** $1 - (5/6)^n$. ($n=4 \to 0.518$, just favourable.)
- **A coin, n flips, at least one head:** $1 - (1/2)^n$. ($n=3 \to 7/8$.)
- **Two dice, 24 rolls, at least one double-six:** $1 - (35/36)^{24} \approx 0.491$, just **un**favourable.

**de Méré's paradox:** betting on a 6 in 4 single-die rolls wins ($0.518$), but a double-six in 24 two-die rolls loses ($0.491$) — even though the naive ratio $4/6 = 24/36$ suggests they should be equal. The exponent, not the ratio, decides it.

**The trap:** the *probability* of at least one success is **not** the *expected number* of successes. Rolling a die 6 times, the expected count of 6s is $6 \times \tfrac16 = 1$ (linearity of expectation), while the probability of **at least one** 6 is $1 - (5/6)^6 \approx 0.665$. A probability can never exceed 1; an expected count can.

cards.term: You roll a fair six-sided die 6 times. What is the probability of getting at least one 6?
cards.definition: Complement: P(at least one 6) = 1 − P(no 6) = 1 − (5/6)^6 = 1 − 15625/46656 ≈ 1 − 0.335 = 0.665, so ≈66.5%. The tempting 100% confuses this with the EXPECTED number of 6s (6 × 1/6 = 1, a count, not a probability). 33.5% is P(no 6) — the complement itself, not the answer. 16.7% ≈ 1/6 is one single roll; 50% is just a guess.

cards.term: You roll a fair die 6 times. What is the EXPECTED number of 6s you will roll?
cards.definition: By linearity of expectation, write the count as a sum of 6 indicator variables, each with mean 1/6; the expected count is 6 × 1/6 = 1 regardless of dependence. This is deliberately contrasted with the ≈66.5% PROBABILITY of at least one 6: an expected count (1) and a probability (0.665) are different quantities — 0.665 is the wrong answer precisely because it answers the previous question. '6' would require every roll to be a 6.

cards.term: You roll a fair die 4 times. What is the probability of rolling NO 6 at all?
cards.definition: Independent rolls: P(no 6) = (5/6)^4 = 625/1296 ≈ 0.482, so ≈48.2%. 51.8% is its complement, P(at least one 6) — the answer to the NEXT question, not this one. 16.7% ≈ 1/6 is a single roll; 33.3% and 66.7% (≈1/3, 2/3) don't arise here.

cards.term: You roll a fair die 4 times. What is the probability of at least one 6? (This is the bet that made the gambler de Méré famous.)
cards.definition: 1 − (5/6)^4 = 1 − 625/1296 ≈ 0.518, so ≈51.8% — just above one half, so betting on at least one 6 in four rolls is (slightly) favourable. 48.2% is P(no 6), the complement. The naive '4/6 ≈ 66.7%' wrongly multiplies a per-roll chance by the number of rolls (that gives the expected count, 4 × 1/6 = 2/3, not a probability).

cards.term: What is the smallest number of rolls of a fair die for which the probability of getting at least one 6 first exceeds 50%?
cards.definition: We need the smallest n with 1 − (5/6)^n > 0.5, i.e. (5/6)^n < 0.5. (5/6)^3 = 125/216 ≈ 0.579 (so 3 rolls give only 0.421 < 0.5), while (5/6)^4 = 625/1296 ≈ 0.482 (so 4 rolls give 0.518 > 0.5). Hence 4 is the tipping point. (Not 'the expected wait' 6, a common wrong instinct.)

cards.term: You roll a pair of fair dice 24 times. What is the probability of getting at least one double-six (a 6 on both dice simultaneously)?
cards.definition: A double-six has probability 1/36 per throw, so P(at least one in 24) = 1 − (35/36)^24 ≈ 1 − 0.509 = 0.491, ≈49.1% — just UNfavourable. This is the heart of de Méré's paradox: 4 single-die rolls is favourable (0.518) yet 24 two-die rolls is not, even though 4/6 = 24/36. 50.9% is the complement P(no double-six); 25 rolls would tip the bet over 50%.

cards.term: You flip a fair coin 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?
cards.definition: P(at least one head) = 1 − P(all tails) = 1 − (1/2)^3 = 1 − 1/8 = 7/8. The only excluded outcome is TTT. 1/8 is P(all tails) itself; 3/8 is P(exactly one head) = C(3,1)/8; 1/2 and 3/4 don't match.

cards.term: You flip a fair coin 10 times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?
cards.definition: 1 − (1/2)^10 = 1 − 1/1024 = 1023/1024 ≈ 0.999 — almost certain; the only excluded outcome is all ten tails. 511/512 = 1 − (1/2)^9 is the answer for nine flips; 1/1024 is P(all tails); 10/1024 = C(10,1)/1024 is P(exactly one head).

cards.term: What is the standard trick for computing the probability of 'at least one' success in a series of independent trials?
choices: Use the expected number of successes directly
Multiply the probabilities of each trial
Add the probabilities of each trial succeeding
Take the probability of exactly one success
Compute 1 minus the probability of zero successes (the complement)
cards.definition: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none). Adding per-trial probabilities double-counts overlaps (and can exceed 1); multiplying them gives P(all succeed); the expected count and 'exactly one' answer different questions. The complement turns a messy union into a single clean product, P(none) = ∏(1 − pᵢ).

cards.term: You roll two fair dice. What is the probability that at least one of them shows an even number?
cards.definition: Complement again: P(at least one even) = 1 − P(both odd). Each die is odd with probability 1/2, so P(both odd) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4 and the answer is 1 − 1/4 = 3/4. 1/2 is the chance a single die is even; 1/4 is P(both odd) itself.
